quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 2, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Pathogens: Transmission, Treatment, and Disease Examples
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Antibiotic Resistance, Toxin Production, Pathogen Transmission, Obligate Intracellular Pathogens, Viruses, Bacteria, Fungi, Helminths, Prions, Malaria

questions.question: Which biological characteristic explains why standard antibiotic treatments fail to eliminate viral infections?
options: Viruses undergo rapid genetic shifts that outpace the drug.
Viruses lack specific cellular targets affected by the drug.
Viruses possess a physical size that is far too microscopic.
Viruses consist of complex multicellular tissue structures.
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial structures like cell walls, ribosomes, and metabolic pathways. Viruses lack these cellular targets because they are acellular and use host cell machinery. Antibiotics cannot distinguish between viral and host cell processes.

questions.question: Which pathogen causes disease by producing toxins?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are the primary pathogens that cause disease through toxin production. Examples include Clostridium tetani (tetanus toxin), Clostridium botulinum (botulinum toxin), and Vibrio cholerae (cholera toxin). Viruses cause disease by destroying host cells, protozoa by tissue invasion, and fungi by allergic reactions or tissue damage.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a mode of pathogen transmission?
questions.explanation: Genetic inheritance involves passing genes from parents to offspring, not pathogens. Pathogens can be transmitted via vectors (mosquitoes), sexual contact (STIs), and airborne droplets (coughing/sneezing).

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: All pathogens are parasites because they live at the expense of their host. However, not all parasites are pathogens - some cause no harm. Pathogens can be unicellular (bacteria, protozoa) or acellular (viruses, prions), not always multicellular.

questions.question: Pathogens that can only replicate inside host cells are called:
questions.explanation: Obligate intracellular pathogens, such as viruses and some bacteria (Chlamydia), cannot reproduce outside host cells. Opportunistic pathogens cause disease only in immunocompromised hosts. Facultative anaerobes can live with or without oxygen. Commensals live harmlessly on or in the body.

questions.question: Which pathogen type is responsible for diseases like influenza and COVID-19?
questions.explanation: Influenza and COVID-19 are both caused by viruses (influenza virus and SARS-CoV-2). Bacteria cause infections like strep throat and tuberculosis. Fungi cause athlete's foot and ringworm. Protozoa cause malaria and amoebic dysentery.

questions.question: Bacteria such as Streptococcus and Salmonella are examples of:
questions.explanation: Streptococcus and Salmonella are bacteria, which are prokaryotic pathogens (lacking membrane-bound nucleus). They are not viruses, eukaryotic parasites, or prions.

questions.question: Which fungal pathogen commonly causes ringworm and athlete's foot?
questions.explanation: Dermatophytes are fungi that infect skin, hair, and nails, causing ringworm (tinea corporis) and athlete's foot (tinea pedis). Aspergillus causes respiratory infections, Candida causes yeast infections, and Histoplasma causes histoplasmosis.

questions.question: Parasitic worms such as roundworms and tapeworms are classified as:
questions.explanation: Helminths are parasitic worms, including roundworms (nematodes) and tapeworms (cestodes). They are multicellular eukaryotes. Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotes, fungi are eukaryotic but not worms, and bacteria are prokaryotic.

questions.question: Which is an example of a prion disease?
questions.explanation: Mad cow disease (bovine spongiform encephalopathy - BSE) is a prion disease caused by misfolded proteins that damage brain tissue. Tuberculosis is bacterial, ringworm is fungal, and malaria is protozoan.

questions.question: Which treatment is typically used for helminth infections?
questions.explanation: Helminths (parasitic worms) are treated with antiparasitic drugs (also called antihelminthics) such as albendazole, mebendazole, and ivermectin. Antibiotics target bacteria, antivirals target viruses, and antifungals target fungal infections.

questions.question: Prions are infectious agents composed primarily of:
questions.explanation: Prions are infectious proteins - misfolded versions of normal cellular proteins that cause other proteins to misfold. They contain no genetic material (DNA or RNA). They cause diseases like mad cow disease (BSE) and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease.

questions.question: Which pathogen is transmitted by mosquitoes and causes malaria?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium is a protozoan parasite transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes, causing malaria. E. coli is a bacterium causing food poisoning, influenza is a virus, and Candida is a fungus causing yeast infections.

questions.question: All of the following infections are airborne except:
questions.explanation: Cholera is transmitted through contaminated water or food (fecal-oral route), not through the air. Tuberculosis (bacteria), influenza (virus), and measles (virus) are all transmitted via airborne droplets.

questions.question: Malaria parasite is ____________
questions.explanation: The malaria parasite (Plasmodium) is a protozoan - a single-celled eukaryote. It is not a bacterium, virus, or fungus. It has a complex life cycle involving mosquitoes and humans.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered pathogen transmission and disease examples! 🦠
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between obligate intracellular pathogens and others.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on transmission modes and specific pathogen examples.
scoreMessages.0: Please review why antibiotics don't work on viruses, toxin-producing bacteria, and vector-borne diseases.
